mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the best case scenario?
mcqs.explanation: In the best case scenario (when the array is already sorted), Insertion Sort runs in O(n) time because it only needs to make one comparison per element without any shifts.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is true about Insertion Sort?

options.text: It is not a stable sorting algorithm
It is efficient for large datasets
It is adaptive and performs well on nearly sorted data

mcqs.explanation: Insertion Sort is adaptive, meaning it performs efficiently on nearly sorted data. It is also stable and in-place (requires O(1) additional space).

mcqs.question: In Insertion Sort, what happens when the key element is greater than all elements in the sorted portion?

options.text: It is placed at the beginning of the sorted portion
It is placed at the end of the sorted portion

mcqs.explanation: If the key element is greater than all elements in the sorted portion, it remains in its current position (which becomes the new end of the sorted portion).

mcqs.question: Which sorting algorithm uses Insertion Sort as a base case for small subarrays?
mcqs.explanation: Timsort, which is used in Python's built-in sort and Java's Arrays.sort(), uses Insertion Sort for small subarrays (typically when n < 64).

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of Insertion Sort?
mcqs.explanation: Insertion Sort is an in-place sorting algorithm, meaning it requires only a constant amount of additional space (O(1)).
